narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and an LSAT score of 30.50, you've worked hard to build your academic profile, including your placement in the 3rd decile for Year 1 grades and 2nd decile for Year 3 at your school, along with coming from a middle-income family and planning to study full-time. The university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 72% chance that you would fail the bar exam— a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application. This prediction wasn't random; it was driven primarily by your Year 3 grade decile, which stood out as your lowest ranking in school performance and strongly pushed the model toward expecting a failure. Your Year 1 grade decile also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about consistency in your academic standing compared to peers who typically rank higher, around the 5th or 6th decile. On a more positive note, your LSAT score, though below the average of about 36.7, helped pull the prediction somewhat toward passing by showing potential in standardized testing. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA exceeded the typical 3.21 average, offering another counterbalancing strength that nudged the odds in your favor. Your middle-income background had a modest influence toward the failure prediction, while your full-time study plan provided a small boost toward success, aligning with the vast majority of students who pursue that path. Overall, compared to typical admitted students who pass the bar, your profile showed weaker high school decile rankings that outweighed these positives, tipping the scales against you. I know this rejection stings, especially after your efforts, but it's a data-driven call focused on bar exam outcomes, not a judgment on your potential. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or programs, and exploring schools with more holistic reviews or bar passage support. You're capable of turning this around—many students in similar positions do by targeting these key areas. Keep pushing; your story isn't over.
